skill.name: safe-debug
skill.description: Conservative deep learning debugging skill. Diagnoses training failures, CUDA OOM, checkpoint errors, shape mismatches, and NaN losses before suggesting minimal patches.
skill.summary: Rigor Debug / Rigor Audit skill for conservative diagnosis of deep learning training and inference failures.
skill.author: lllllllama

security_audit.summary: The static analyzer flagged 22 patterns as potential security issues, but all are false positives after manual review. The 'Ruby/shell backtick execution' detections are triggered by inline code formatting in markdown (backticks around filenames like `debug_outputs/DIAGNOSIS.md`). The 'Weak cryptographic algorithm' flags at SKILL.md lines 3 and 18 match on the word 'safe' in the skill name/description. The 'Path traversal sequence' flags are on relative file paths in documentation. The Python script performs only string classification, JSON output, and local file writes — no network, no eval, no subprocess execution. The skill is a conservative debug aid that explicitly avoids automatic patching.

low_findings.title: File output to user-specified directory
low_findings.description: The script writes diagnosis, patch plan, and status files to a user-specified output directory (default: debug_outputs/). The path is resolved with Path.resolve() but no sandboxing or path validation is applied. This is a standard CLI pattern and not a security risk for a local debugging tool, but noted for transparency.

content.user_title: Debug deep learning training failures safely
content.value_statement: Deep learning training failures waste hours of GPU time and research momentum. This skill provides conservative root-cause diagnosis for CUDA OOM, checkpoint errors, shape mismatches, and NaN losses — suggesting only minimal patches after explicit researcher approval.
actual_capabilities: Classifies error tracebacks into categories: CUDA OOM, checkpoint mismatch, distributed issues, device mismatch, shape mismatch, NaN loss, and file not found.
Generates a structured DIAGNOSIS.md with error excerpt, category, and conservative analysis.
Produces a PATCH_PLAN.md with the smallest suggested fix and a savepoint recommendation flag.
Outputs a status.json file with patch authorization status (always False until human approval).
Runs as a CLI tool with --error-file, --error-text, --output-dir, and --json flags for flexible integration.
Explicitly refuses to modify repository code without explicit researcher approval.
limitations: Classification is keyword-based and may misclassify novel or uncommon error patterns.
Only provides diagnosis and suggestions — never automatically applies patches.
Does not connect to live training runs or read GPU state in real time.
Does not handle non-Python ML frameworks or non-deep-learning errors.

use_cases.title: Diagnose CUDA out-of-memory errors during training
use_cases.description: Paste a CUDA OOM traceback and receive a categorized diagnosis with configuration-first suggestions (batch size, mixed precision) before any model code is touched.
use_cases.target_user: ML research engineers running large model training

use_cases.title: Investigate checkpoint load failures
use_cases.description: Get a conservative root-cause analysis when a pretrained checkpoint fails to load, with suggestions to verify model variant and strictness before adding compatibility code.
use_cases.target_user: Researchers resuming work from saved checkpoints

use_cases.title: Debug NaN loss in training loops
use_cases.description: Receive categorized diagnosis and controlled-experiment suggestions when training loss becomes NaN, separating data, precision, and learning rate hypotheses.
use_cases.target_user: PhD students and applied scientists debugging unstable training

prompt_templates.title: Basic error diagnosis
prompt_templates.prompt:
Diagnose this deep learning error conservatively. Show me the category, likely cause, and the smallest safe fix.

Error:
```
[PASTE TRACEBACK HERE]
```
prompt_templates.scenario: First-time use with a simple traceback

prompt_templates.title: Checkpoint mismatch investigation
prompt_templates.prompt: I am loading a pretrained checkpoint and getting a size mismatch error. Use the safe-debug skill to classify this, explain the likely cause, and suggest the minimal fix. Do not propose code changes yet.
prompt_templates.scenario: Resuming from a checkpoint with a model variant mismatch

prompt_templates.title: NaN loss controlled run
prompt_templates.prompt: My training loss went to NaN after step 500. Use safe-debug to diagnose the category, suggest a short controlled run to isolate startup vs. mid-training NaN, and recommend a savepoint before any patch.
prompt_templates.scenario: Mid-training NaN with need for safe rollback

prompt_templates.title: Multi-error batch triage
prompt_templates.prompt: I have three separate error tracebacks from a distributed training run: a shape mismatch, a distributed launch error, and a NaN loss. Use safe-debug to classify each, prioritize them by severity, and generate a combined patch plan with explicit approval gates.

output_examples.input: Error: RuntimeError: CUDA out of memory. Tried to allocate 2.00 GiB.
output_examples.output: Category: cuda_oom. Patch authorized: False. Savepoint recommended: False. The system detected a CUDA out-of-memory condition. Suggested actions: Check effective batch size, input resolution, and mixed-precision settings before patching model code. Prefer a configuration-only reduction before touching architecture.

output_examples.input: Error: RuntimeError: Error(s) in loading state_dict for ResNet50: size mismatch for fc.weight: copying a param of shape [1000, 2048] in CKPT, the shape in current model is [10, 2048].
output_examples.output: Category: checkpoint_mismatch. Patch authorized: False. Savepoint recommended: True. The checkpoint was saved with a different output head (1000 classes) than the current model (10 classes). Suggested actions: Verify checkpoint source, model variant, and load strictness assumptions. Confirm whether the mismatch is expected before introducing compatibility code.

best_practices: Always provide the full traceback or error text, not just a summary, for accurate classification.
Use --json flag first to preview the diagnosis before writing output files to disk.
Treat the patch plan as a proposal — apply changes only after explicit researcher approval.
Create a savepoint or branch before applying any medium-risk or high-risk suggested fix.
anti_patterns: Do not use this skill for broad refactoring or speculative code adaptation — it is strictly for active failure diagnosis.
Do not apply patches suggested by the skill without reading the full patch plan and confirming scope.
Do not use this skill for general repository familiarization or exploratory code reading.

faq.question: What types of errors does this skill classify?
faq.answer: The skill classifies errors into seven categories: CUDA OOM, checkpoint mismatch, distributed issues, device mismatch, shape mismatch, NaN loss, and file not found. It also has a general runtime failure category and an unknown fallback.

faq.question: Does this skill automatically apply fixes?
faq.answer: No. The skill explicitly refuses to modify repository code. It generates a diagnosis and a patch plan, but patch_authorized is always False until a human researcher explicitly approves the change.

faq.question: How is this different from a normal code review tool?
faq.answer: This skill is purpose-built for deep learning research failures. It understands domain-specific error patterns like CUDA OOM, DDP launch issues, and mixed-precision NaN loss, and it prioritizes configuration fixes over model code changes.

faq.question: Can I use this skill for non-PyTorch frameworks?
faq.answer: The skill is optimized for PyTorch-style tracebacks and deep learning research workflows. It may misclassify errors from other frameworks or general software bugs outside the ML domain.

faq.question: What output files does the skill generate?
faq.answer: The skill writes three files to the output directory: DIAGNOSIS.md (category and error excerpt), PATCH_PLAN.md (suggested actions and savepoint flag), and status.json (machine-readable status with patch authorization set to False).

faq.question: Is the skill safe to run in my repository?
faq.answer: Yes. The script only reads an error file, classifies text, and writes output files to a specified directory. It does not execute subprocesses, access the network, or modify existing repository code.
